medications.indication_rationale: A potent topical corticosteroid suppresses the acute inflammatory contact reaction and is the source note's prescribed treatment. In telehealth this is initiated by the assessing clinician after decontamination and in-person review; apply only to affected scalp once residual product has been rinsed off.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer to the affected scalp twice daily to dry skin (dose per source note; do not exceed)
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not apply over frankly infected or broken/denuded skin if secondary infection develops
Apply only after the offending product has been rinsed off

medications.indication_rationale: Adjunct for the itch and allergic component of the reaction, per the source note. It does not treat a chemical burn or infection and is not a substitute for decontamination or in-person assessment.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 10 mg orally once daily (dose per source note)

medications.drug_name: Continued or repeated use of the unregulated hair-growth product
medications.indication_rationale: The product is the cause of the injury — it must be discarded, not re-applied. Any advice that permits continued use is a dangerous error of commission.
No fever, no spreading erythema and no purulence — there is no secondary infection to treat. Prescribing antibiotics for serous exudate is over-treatment; reserve for documented secondary infection.
Not warranted for a localised contact reaction without systemic or airway involvement; the definitive steps are decontamination, a potent topical steroid and in-person assessment.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for and act on swelling of the lips/tongue/face, throat tightness or breathing difficulty, spreading redness, fever, or thick foul-smelling pus.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These are the escalation triggers that convert a localised reaction into an emergency or a secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Discard the unregulated online product immediately and avoid unregulated/internet-purchased topical compounds; use only TGA-approved treatments for hair loss discussed with a GP.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Prevents re-exposure and recurrence, and steers the patient to evidence-based, regulated options.

patient_education_points.point: The reaction is caused by the product you applied. The single most important step right now is to rinse it all off with plenty of cool water and stop using it.
Because the depth of a chemical skin injury can't be judged over a call, you need to be seen in person today to check how deep it is and start treatment.
A topical steroid will calm the inflammation and an antihistamine helps the itch, but the product must never be used again.
Get emergency help immediately if your lips, tongue, face or throat swell, if you have any trouble breathing, or if the clear weeping turns into thick, foul-smelling pus.
Your existing hair-thinning pattern will not change once the skin heals; discuss safe, approved options for hair loss with your GP.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent same-day in-person assessment today, then GP review in 2-3 days once the acute injury has been seen
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: resolution of the dermatitis, skin integrity and injury depth, any secondary infection, and safe TGA-approved options for hair loss
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: airway/facial swelling or breathing difficulty, spreading redness, fever, or purulent discharge — seek emergency care

must_include_items: Recognise the acute product-induced scalp injury (contact dermatitis / possible chemical injury) rather than anchoring on the chronic hair loss
Instruct immediate patient-performed decontamination — rinse the scalp with copious cool/lukewarm water and stop using the product
Actively screen for and exclude airway/systemic allergic (anaphylaxis) features
Arrange URGENT same-day in-person assessment because chemical-injury depth cannot be graded remotely
Provide safety-netting with explicit 000 escalation for any airway/breathing symptoms
acceptable_alternatives: Recommending a potent topical corticosteroid and an oral antihistamine as adjuncts is appropriate
Equally acceptable to defer the steroid prescription to the in-person assessor rather than prescribing sight-unseen
errors_of_omission: Failing to instruct decontamination / to stop the product
Failing to screen for airway or anaphylaxis features
Failing to arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (under-triaging to routine care)
Failing to safety-net for airway swelling and secondary infection
errors_of_commission: Advising continued or repeated use of the product
Anchoring on androgenetic alopecia and reassuring the patient that it is only a hair-loss problem
Prescribing empiric oral antibiotics with no signs of infection
Prescribing systemic corticosteroids for a localised reaction
Reflexively escalating a systemically well patient with no airway features straight to 000/ED
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) decontamination advice, (b) an airway/anaphylaxis screen with an explicit 000 safety-net, and (c) arranging urgent same-day in-person assessment. Anchoring on the chronic hair loss and dismissing the acute injury, or omitting the in-person assessment, is a critical failure regardless of other content.
